coredata.dc:title: Divulging the contrasting potential of sustainable starch based bioplastic films derived from Zea mays and Colocasia esculenta incorporated with chitosan and CQDs: detailed characterization and efficient role in lychee fruit packaging
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Kour, Manmeet
Chaudhary, Savita
Kumar, Rajeev
coredata.dc:description: The formulation of biodegradable plastic from sustainable and underexploited reserves considered as the most probable substitute to commercially used petroleum based plastic materials. Herein, contrasting potential of sustainable starch based bioplastic derived from Zea mays and Colocasia esculenta have been carried out in detail. The formed bioplastics were further customized via the inclusion of chitosan and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as a copolymer and functional filler to facilitate the properties of starch bioplastic film in packaging. The incorporation of chitosan and CQDs significantly enhanced mechanical properties, with CES@CHI@CQDs exhibiting superior flexibility (elongation at break: 680.58 %) and ZMS@CHI@CQDs demonstrating maximum tensile strength (21.53 MPa) and Young's modulus (41 MPa), attributed to strong polymer-CQD interactions. Hydrophobicity improved with increased water contact angles, reaching 103.25° (CES@CHI@CQDs) and 115.4° (ZMS@CHI@CQDs), enhancing water resistance and durability. Antioxidant properties were significantly boosted, with radical scavenging activity reaching 34 % and 35 %, respectively, due to CQD incorporation. Antibacterial assays confirmed enhanced inhibition against E. coli and S. aureus, with inhibition zones up to 4 mm. The loam burial test validated their biodegradability, supporting carbon footprint reduction. Additionally, antibacterial, antioxidative, moisture resistance, and UV-blocking properties confirmed their potential for sustainable packaging applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Zea mays
Colocasia esculenta
Bioplastic
Biodegradable
Food packaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Extraction of starch and synthesis of CQDs from Colocasia esculenta
Water vapour transmission rate of bioplastic films
Potential aptitude of developed films for packaging applications
Structural and morphological characterization of bioplastic films
Thermal properties of CES@CHI@CQDs and ZMS@CHI@CQDs
Mechanical properties of CES@CHI@CQDs and ZMS@CHI@CQDs
Moisture content and water vapour permeability rate of CES@CHI@CQDs and ZMS@CHI@CQDs
Water contact angle (WCA) of CES@CHI@CQDs and ZMS@CHI@CQD based films
Antibacterial activities of bioplastic film films
Antioxidant activities of CES@CHI@CQDs and ZMS@CHI@CQD based films
Biodegradability studies for of CES@CHI@CQDs and ZMS@CHI@CQD films

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Manmeet Kour is thankful to Maulana Azad National Fellowship (MANF). Savita Chaudhary is thankful to DST Inspire Faculty award [ IFACH- 17 ], Haryana State Council for Science and Technology [ HSCSIT/R&D/2020/476 ], DST Chandigarh [ S&T&RE/RP/147/e-2873/(19-20)/Sanc/12/2019/1878-1885 ], and [ S&T&RE/RP/147/e-2873/(22-23)/Sanc/09/2022/907-915 ] and DST Purse grants II for financial assistance. Rajeev Kumar would like to acknowledge the support of UGC , India under the INDO-US 21st Century knowledge Initiative project [F.No. 194-2/2016 (IC) ]. The authors are thankful to the Sophisticated Analytical Instrumentation Facility (SAIF, CIL), Panjab University, Chandigarh for technical support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration showing the fabrication of Colocasia esculenta/Zea mays starch derived bioplastic film with further modification with CQDs and chitosan.
FTIR and XRD spectra of (a,c) Colocasia esculenta and (b,d) Zea mays starch derived bioplastic film with further modification with CQDs and chitosan.
FESEM images of (a-d) Colocasia esculenta and (e-h) Zea mays starch derived bioplastic film with further modification with CQDs and chitosan.

(a) TGA, (b) stress vs. strain graph, (c, d) tensile strength and (e,f) Elongation at break graph for Colocasia esculenta and Zea mays starch derived bioplastic film and their bioplastic films with CQDs and chitosan.
(a) Moisture Content and (b) WVTR for Colocasia esculenta and Zea mays starch derived bioplastic film and their bioplastic films with CQDs and chitosan.
Water contact angle analysis for (a) CES, (b) CES@CHI (c) CES@CQDs (d) CES@CHI@CQDs (e) ZMS (f) ZMS@CHI (g) ZMS@CQDs and (h) ZMS@CHI@CQDs.

Antibacterial activity for Colocasia esculenta and Zea mays starch derived bioplastic film and their bioplastic films with CQDs and chitosan against gram negative and gram positive bacterial strains i.e. E.coli and S.aureus.

(a) UV–vis. Absorbance spectra (b) fluorescence emission spectra as a function of excitation wavelength (c) UV transmittance spectra of CQDs obtained from the waste slurry, and (d) DPPH radical scavenging activity of Colocasia esculenta and Zea mays starch derived bioplastic film and their bioplastic films with CQDs and chitosan.
The release rate of the CQDs from the Starch/Chitosan based films in various food simulant solutions (a) Water (b) 10 % Ethanol (c) 50 % Ethanol (d) 90 % Ethanol.
The performance of CES@CHI@CQDs and ZMS@CHI@CQDs based bioplastic film towards the preservation of highly perishable lychee fruit.

head.ce:title: Divulging the contrasting potential of sustainable starch based bioplastic films derived from Zea mays and Colocasia esculenta incorporated with chitosan and CQDs: detailed characterization and efficient role in lychee fruit packaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Centre of Advanced Studies in Chemistry, Panjab University, Chandigarh 160014, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Centre of Advanced Studies in Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry and Centre of Advanced Studies in Chemistry, Panjab University, Chandigarh 160014, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Environment Studies, Panjab University, Chandigarh 160014, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Environment Studies, Panjab University, Chandigarh 160014, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The formulation of biodegradable plastic from sustainable and underexploited reserves considered as the most probable substitute to commercially used petroleum based plastic materials. Herein, contrasting potential of sustainable starch based bioplastic derived from Zea mays and Colocasia esculenta have been carried out in detail. The formed bioplastics were further customized via the inclusion of chitosan and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as a copolymer and functional filler to facilitate the properties of starch bioplastic film in packaging. The incorporation of chitosan and CQDs significantly enhanced mechanical properties, with CES@CHI@CQDs exhibiting superior flexibility (elongation at break: 680.58 %) and ZMS@CHI@CQDs demonstrating maximum tensile strength (21.53 MPa) and Young's modulus (41 MPa), attributed to strong polymer-CQD interactions. Hydrophobicity improved with increased water contact angles, reaching 103.25° (CES@CHI@CQDs) and 115.4° (ZMS@CHI@CQDs), enhancing water resistance and durability. Antioxidant properties were significantly boosted, with radical scavenging activity reaching 34 % and 35 %, respectively, due to CQD incorporation. Antibacterial assays confirmed enhanced inhibition against E. coli and S. aureus, with inhibition zones up to 4 mm. The loam burial test validated their biodegradability, supporting carbon footprint reduction. Additionally, antibacterial, antioxidative, moisture resistance, and UV-blocking properties confirmed their potential for sustainable packaging applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The extensive application of plastic based material in food packing, agriculture, industrial, pharmaceutical and construction sector has created the ecological nuisance [1]. The low synthetic cost, high durable nature and ease in inhibiting infection have further supported their extensive usage in packing diverse range of food products [2]. The efficient thermal and mechanical characteristics of plastics have further enhanced their potential usage in producing consumer goods and electronics products [3]. Hence, there is an exponential increment in the production of plastic based materials in last few decades. By the end of 2025, approximately 8 billion metric tons of plastic will be found in natural environments, landfills, and oceans [4]. The available data indicates that the enhancement in the rate of health diseases including asthma, bronchitis, skin diseases, and many other neurological disorders linked with plastics [5]. Commercially, incineration and recycling are the two main ways to dispose of these plastics menace [6]. However, the release of toxic gases during incineration and challenging task of sorting process during recycling method has restricted their widespread usage in tackling plastic-based pollution [7]. The persistent use of hazardous chemicals including phthalates, furans, dioxins and many other carcinogenic components caused severe health issue among human beings [8]. The untreated dumped plastic packaging is one of the primary sources of pollution in landfills which ultimately profaned water and soil resources. Thus, plastic menace has posed critical issue among researchers to find renewable, biodegradable and sustainable alternatives with similar characteristic properties to mitigate ecological concerns [9]. Therefore, the current work proposed the synthesis of cost effective and eco-friendly means to fabricate biofilms for food packaging application using sustainable precursor source as an effective alternative for commercially used plastics.

Up to now, bioplastic production has been achieved via using different types of starch, proteins, chitin and fats-based precursor sources to develop cost effective, biocompatible, biodegradable polymers films [10]. Among all, starch-based materials constitute one of the most widely used bio-polymers, garnering a lot of attention from the scientific community [11]. Starch is cost effective, biocompatible, biodegradable and abundant biopolymer [12–14]. The flexible nature of developed biodegradable films have the ability to form diverse range of consumer-friendly products including gels, bags, capsule coverings, pouches etc. to meet their day-to-day activities [15]. Furthermore, usage of sustainable precursor sources including agricultural and industrial waste to produce bioplastic films have further improved their cost benefits, scalability and quality [16]. Recently, starch-based films have also been employed in packaging applications [17]. However, the inherent hygroscopic nature and inferior mechanical properties of neat starch based biopolymers have restricted their usage in wider range of applications and have compelled to explore additional filler materials to modulate their properties [18–22]. For instance, metal based fillers like lithium perchlorate has been added in the starch based bio-polymer films to modify their electrical conductivity [23]. The high surface area with superior electrical and mechanical properties of graphene have also been used along with starch based bioplastic films to enhance their potential in electrochemical based double layer capacitors [24]. The combination of graphene with starch based bioplastic films have also hold significant importance in developing field effect transistors and sensors [25]. Additionally, the application of carbon dots derived from waste biomass resources have also been employed as filler to enhance the structural properties of starch based bio-polymer films [26].

Therefore, in this work we have reported the application of Colocasia esculenta (taro herb) and Zea mays (corn) as a precursor source for the preparation of starch-based bioplastic films. The characteristic properties of bioplastic films were further enhanced with carbon quantum dots (CQDs), which were synthesized from the residual waste slurry of Colocasia esculenta (taro) left after starch extraction. This approach not only improves the functional properties of the films but also aligns with the principles of sustainability and the circular economy by utilizing agricultural waste. The incorporation of CQDs as a filler material enhances the mechanical, antimicrobial, and optical properties of the films while promoting waste valorization and resource efficiency. The antimicrobial efficiency of the developed films has further been modified via adding chitosan in the reaction medium. The contrasting potential of sustainable starch-based bioplastic derived from Zea mays and Colocasia esculenta have been compared in term of their physico-chemical properties including moisture content, thermal stability and water vapour transmission rate. The variations in the compositions of formed films were compared with respect to their mechanical strength and hydrophobic character. The biodegradable nature with excellent mechanical properties of developed film has further been checked for their efficacy in packaging applications of lychee fruit as a model system. The choice of the fruit is based on its highly perishable nature and high propensity towards microbial and oxidative attack, leading to a shorter shelf life [27]. Thus, the antibacterial characteristics and high anti-oxidative properties of developed film in this work have the ability to preserve packaged lychee fruit by limiting the growth of microbes, hence increasing the shelf life. The produced films have also offered sustainable, highly biodegradable, hygienic and environmentally friendly means to pack the food items. This work offers a better framework for developing films from biomass resources with advanced anti-oxidative, antimicrobial and UV barrier properties for packaging applications towards highly perishable items.

Colocasia esculenta were sourced from a local market of Sector 15, Chandigarh, India. Sodium metabisulphite, Zea mays based starch and 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) were attained from Himedia with 99 % purity. Chitosan with lower molecular weight (Deacelylated chitin, Poly (D-glucosamine) and Glycerol was procured from Sigma Aldrich with 99 % purity. All the chemicals were used as such without any kind of processing. The respective sample preparations were done in distilled water. Mueller-Hinton agar (M173) and nutrient broth (M002) were purchased from HiMedia chemicals. Petri plates were obtained from Tarsons (Catalogue no. 460090–90 MM). The gram-positive i.e., S. aureus (MTCC 1430) and Gram-negative i.e., E. coli (MTCC 1610) bacterial cells were procured from the Department of Microbiology, Panjab University, Chandigarh. The lychee fruit was obtained from a local market (Sector 15, Chandigarh, India) for the packaging application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Extraction of starch and synthesis of CQDs from Colocasia esculenta
ce:section.ce:para: Initially, Colocasia esculenta were washed with tap water to remove surface impurities. The cleaned and peeled tubers were blended in a commercial mixer with 0.1 % sodium metabisulfite and stirred magnetically overnight at 500 rpm (Fig. 1 ) [28]. The mixture was then filtered using a double-layered muslin cloth, and the filtrate was centrifuged at 4000 rpm. The supernatant was discarded, while the remaining residue was dried in a conventional oven at 40 °C, ground, and sieved through a 100 μm mesh. The residual biomass from starch extraction was calcined in a muffle furnace at 300 °C for 3 h, then pulverized using a pestle and mortar. The resulting calcined powder (1 g) was dispersed in 500 mL of distilled water for 24 h. The dispersion was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min, and the obtained material was dried to yield CQD powder.

Bioplastic films were synthesized using starch from Colocasia esculenta (CES) and Zea mays (ZMS), incorporating chitosan (CHI) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as modifiers. Initially, 4 % (w/v) starch solution was prepared in distilled water and sonicated for 10 min. Chitosan was dissolved at 1 % (w/v) in 2 % (v/v) aqueous acetic acid under stirring at 50 °C for 2 h. CQDs (4 % w/w relative to starch) were added to the starch solution and ultrasonicated for 5 min for uniform dispersion. Gelatinization was achieved by adding 30 % glycerol (w/w relative to starch) at 85 °C. Air bubbles were removed, and the resulting solutions were cast into petri dishes.

The synthesized films included:
•
CES-based films: CES (pure), CES@CHI, CES@CQDs, and CES@CHI@CQDs.
•
ZMS-based films: ZMS (pure), ZMS@CHI, ZMS@CQDs, and ZMS@CHI@CQDs.

After casting, films were purified in a coagulation bath (acetone, methanol, ethanol), dried, and stored in a humid environment at room temperature for 48 h before further use. The impact of chitosan and CQDs on CES and ZMS films was evaluated for their enhanced functional properties.

The morphological characterization of as prepared CES, CES@CHI, CES@CQDs, CES@CHI@CQDs and ZMS, ZMS@CHI, ZMS@CQDs, ZMS@CHI@CQDs films were evaluated by field emission-scanning electron micrograph (FE-SEM, JEOL JSM 7500F) at an accelerating voltage of 10 kV. Perkin Elmer (RXI) Fourier Transform Infrared spectrometer (FTIR) in the operational range of 4000–400 cm−1 was employed to investigate the functional groups on the multifunctional films. The crystallinity index of all resultant films were assessed using Panalytical X'Pert Pro X-Ray diffraction (XRD) using monochromatic Cukα radiation within working range 2θ = 5–90° and λ = 0.1541 nm. The thermal properties of the developed films were analysed through Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) using SDT Q-600 instrument at a heating rate of 10 °C/min under nitrogen-controlled environment in the range of 25–1000 °C. The optical and photo luminescent properties of the CQDs were measured through LABINDIA UV–Visible (UV–vis.) spectrophotometer and Perkin Elmer LS-55 spectro-fluorometric instrument in working range of 200–800 nm respectively. The mechanical properties of as prepared biofilms were analysed through Biss Universal Testing Machine (UTM) interlinked with computer operating system by calculating tensile strength, young's modulus and elongation at break. The measurements were conducted using Futek load cell of 400 N, crosshead speed of 1 mm/min. A film size of about 2 × 4 cm was taken and thereby recorded the average values for each sample. The water contact angle with films was measured using DSA 10 Mk 2 (Kruess) equipped with image processing system. The samples were prepared using deionised water droplets (5 μL) placed on surface of biofilms. The drop shape analysis system (DSA) was utilized to measure the contact angle values and recording the respective images.

The moisture content of the respective films was determined by measuring the initial weight of the sample (0.5 g) [29]. Thereafter, the respective films were placed in a conventional oven for 24 h at 50 °C. The final weight of the films was recorded and then calculating the moisture content by using following equation:
(1)
Moisture Content =
M o
−
M s
M o
x
100
where Mo represent the initial weight of the sample and Ms represent the final weight of the sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water vapour transmission rate of bioplastic films
ce:section.ce:para:
The water vapour transmission rate was measured using desiccant method by ASTM E96 standard [30]. For the analysis, the chosen glass bottle was loaded with 50 mL distilled water and the surface of the bottle was enclosed with the developed film, ensuring proper sealing to prevent evaporation. The bottle edges were sealed with the Teflon tape followed by paraffin wax. The initial weight of the bottle was recorded (Wi). The sealed bottle was then placed in a desiccant chamber containing calcium carbonate and sealed with silica gel and subsequent weight measurements were taken hourly until a constant weight of the film was achieved. The final weight (Wf) of the sample corresponds to the weight loss after 24 h of exposure.
(2)
Water Vapour Transmission Rate =
W i
−
W f
A
x
T
where Wi is the initial weight of the bottle, Wf is the final weight of the bottle, A is the area of the mouth of bottle and T is the time interval.

The anti-oxidative properties of bioplastic CES, CES@CHI, CES@CQDs, CES@CHI@CQDs and ZMS, ZMS@CHI, ZMS@CQDs, ZMS@CHI@CQDs films were assessed by utilizing the DPPH radical scavenging method [31]. For this, 1 g of film was dissolved in 20 mL of methanol and left undisturbed for 24 h. Following this, the solution was then centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 10 min. Afterwards, 5 mL aliquot of the resultant solution was mixed with 5 mL of methanol solution containing DPPH (50 mg/L). The resulting solution was incubated in the dark for 1 h at room temperature and then its absorbance was measured at 517 nm using a UV–vis. Spectrophotometer. The DPPH scavenging rate was estimated by using the following equation.
(3)
DPPH radical scave n ging rate
%
=
1 −
A s
A DPPH
x
100
where ADPPH and As are the respective absorbance value at 517 nm for the methanol and assay solution of DPPH, respectively.

The disc diffusion method has been employed to check the antibacterial activity against S. aureus and E. coli bacterial strains. For the analysis, the respective bacterial strains were incubated overnight at 37 °C for 24 h in nutrient broth. For the analysis, each bacterial strain was added into 25 mL of nutrient agar solution was poured into a sterilized disposable petri dish and kept aside for 30 min. to solidify. After solidification, 100 μL of overnight incubated bacterial solution was poured on agar plate and spread uniformly with the help of sterilized spreader. The films were cut in the form of disc and then were placed in the agar plate. The petri plates were then sealed properly with parafilm and incubated in the incubator at 37 °C for 24 h. The antibacterial activity was checked by zone of inhibition on agar plates.

The dried films were buried at a depth of 15 cm in soil-filled pots and subjected to a 28 days degradation study by soil burial test. The buried samples were retrieved, cleaned to remove soil residues, and weighed. The weight loss of the samples was determined by measuring their initial weight (W0) before inoculation and their final weight (Wf) after the inoculation process. The percentage weight loss was then calculated using the following equation:
(4)
Weight
l oss
%
=
W 0
−
W f
W 0
x
100 %

The release kinetics of CQDs from the developed CES@CQDs, CES@CHI@CQDs and ZMS@CQDs, ZMS@CHI@CQDs films were evaluated in various food simulants. Square-shaped film samples (2.5 cm × 2.5 cm) were immersed in 20 mL of different food simulant solutions, including water, 10 %, 50 %, and 95 % ethanol, representing aqueous and alcoholic food environments, respectively. The samples were incubated at 25 °C under continuous mild agitation. At predetermined time intervals (0, 30, 60, 90,120, 150 and 180 min), aliquots of 1 mL were withdrawn from each solution, and the absorbance was measured at 395 nm using a UV–vis. Spectrophotometer to monitor the release profile of CQDs (32).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Potential aptitude of developed films for packaging applications
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the potential aptitude of bioplastic films in packaging applications, we have chosen Lychees fruit as the model item. Lychee has a short life time and highly perishable. It is prone to microbial spoilage, making it a good candidate for testing antimicrobial films. The delicate skin of lychee requires protective packaging, making it relevant for evaluating bioplastic. For the analysis, properly washed similar sized lychees with approximately comparable shape and readiness were taken for packaging. Firstly, all the films were treated with ultraviolet radiation for 15 min. to disinfect the sample surface and then used it for the packaging test. After disinfection, the chosen lychees were then individually covered with sterilized, CES@CHI@CQDs, and ZMS@CHI@CQDs film respectively. All samples were kept under 25 °C temperature with 50 % humidity level. The respective variations in the colour and their decay behaviour have been assessed at 1st, 2nd, 3rd, 4th, 5th, 7th and 10th day by taking their digital photographs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and morphological characterization of bioplastic films
ce:section.ce:para: The FTIR spectra of Colocasia esculenta (CES) and its composite films (CES@CHI, CES@CQDs, CES@CHI@CQDs) and Zeya mays (ZMS) with its composite films (ZMS@CHI, ZMS@CQDs, ZMS@CHI@CQDs) is shown in Fig. 2a and b respectively. All the spectra displayed wide adsorption band at 3175–3480 cm−1 corresponds to –OH stretching vibrations and H- bonded -OH stretching in starch molecules [32,33]. Another band observed at 2925–2878 cm−1 was ascribed to -CH2 stretching vibration in methyl group present in starch molecules [33]. The absorption band at 1657 cm−1 was mainly aroused due to the presence of carbonyl and amide group in starch, chitosan and their composite films. The pronounced peaks at ∼1155 cm−1 were mainly aroused due to the C − O stretching vibrations which corresponded to the starch and chitosan polymer blend structure [33]. The peak present at 1013 cm−1 corresponds to CO stretching vibration of the C-O-C groups in glucose units [28]. The peak at 846 cm−1 described the CC stretching vibration. The existence of hydroxyl and carbonyl group indicates that the films had excellent biodegradable properties. Furthermore, the reason for the similar IR spectra for CES and ZEM based bioplastic films are due to the overlapping of O − H, C − H, CC, and CO stretching vibrations in both types of starch molecules [32]. There is negligible shifting in frequency after adding CQDs indicates that there is a uniform dispersion of CQDs in the film forming solution [32].

The crystallographic structure of the developed films was assessed by XRD analysis (Fig. 2c and d). The CES and its composite films displayed broad peak positioned at 2θ = 17.1° which further supported the amorphous crystalline nature of the samples. The amorphous crystalline nature further verified the high degree of crystallinity in the developed films. This could be explained by the A type crystals of starch [34]. However, addition of CHI and CQDs has not displayed any significant variations in the crystalline structure of CES. On the other hand, ZES and its composite films have displayed broad peak at 2θ ∼ 20°. The amorphous nature in the developed starch based films is mainly explained due to the retrogradation during the casting process. This retrogradation has further enhanced the chain mobility and decreased the crystalline structure. Moreover, during the casting method, the film forming solution was heated until its gelatinization, which will further disrupt the crystallinity in starch molecules. In addition, this amorphous nature of developed films made them flexible and soft, which further supported their potential application in food packaging.

The morphological characteristics of the Colocasia esculenta/Zea mays starch and their composite films were analysed by FESEM analysis (Fig. 3a). Colocasia esculenta (CES) exhibit a smooth and homogenous surface without any micropores and cracks. The reason for the smooth surface is the hydrogen bonding among starch molecules at high temperature and pressure conditions. However, the morphological characteristic of Zea mays (ZMS) is different with respect to CES. This morphological variation is mainly explained due to their differences in the granular size. i.e. CES possessed the granular size of 1 μm, whereas the granular size in ZMS is 10 μm. However, the incorporation of chitosan in the Colocasia esculenta/Zea mays starch based films has produced the curvier and rough surface in the developed films. This is mainly explained due to the action of Chitosan molecules as a reinforcement agent in starch films. These Chitosan molecules have the ability to fill the empty spaces in Colocasia esculenta/Zea mays starch based biofilm, ultimately helps in increasing the density of modified bioplastic film. Contrarily, the CQDs infused films have not displayed any type of cracks in their surface morphology. These outcomes further support the good compatibility between Colocasia esculenta/Zea mays starch based biofilm with CQDs. This compact structure arises due to hydrogen bonding between polymer matrix and CQDs. Moreover, the small white lump on the surface of CES@CQDs, CES@CHI@CQDs, ZMS@CQDs and ZMS@CHI@CQDs was mainly due to the existence of small sized CQDs in these bioplastic films. However, on comparing the two types of starch based films, it has been observed that the Zea mays starch based biofilm have possessed uniform distribution and more compactness in comparison to Colocasia esculenta based films.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal properties of CES@CHI@CQDs and ZMS@CHI@CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The thermal properties of Colocasia esculenta and Zea mays starch derived bioplastic film with further modification with CQDs and chitosan were estimating by TGA analysis (Fig. 4a) The TGA thermogram of respective films have shown two stage degradation curve. In case of CES and its composite films, the first degradation curve have shown an initial mass loss of (CES = 26 %) in the temperature range of 25–210 °C. However, CES@CHI@CQDs has displayed mass loss of 16 % in temperature range of 25–210 °C. This mass loss was mainly related with the moisture loss from the films. On comparing the two, it was observed that the CES@CHI@CQDs is more hydrophobic and adsorbed less moisture over its surface in comparison to CES film. The second degradation curve was detected in between 210 and 350 °C. In this temperature range, CES film has shown a weight percentage loss of 88 % whereas CES@CHI@CQDs has shown a loss of 70 %. This weight loss is mainly due to disintegration of polymer matrix. The primary reason for this result is the higher level of interaction between the polymer matrix and the CQDs. This enhanced interaction contributes to the increased stability of the CES@CHI@CQDs, providing greater thermal stability.

Likewise, for ZMS, the initial mass loss was 22 % and 12 % in ZMS@CHI@CQDs based films in the temperature range of 25–210 °C. This result indicated that, within this temperature range, the ZMS@CHI@CQDs bioplastic film exhibits the best performance and the highest hydrophobicity. The second degradation curve was found between 210 and 350 °C. In this range ZMS displays the loss in weight of 83 %, however, ZMS@CHI@CQDs exhibit the weight loss of 68 %. This weight loss is because of degradation of polymer matrix. On comparing the data, it was found that the ZMS@CHI@CQDs based films exhibited higher level of stability and supported their potential utilities in food packaging application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanical properties of CES@CHI@CQDs and ZMS@CHI@CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The mechanical properties are critical for maintaining the product's integrity and durability throughout its lifecycle. The parameters considered were tensile strength, young's modulus and elongation at break. Tensile strength refers to the maximum amount of stress (load) a material can withstand without being breaking. Young's modulus refers to the ratio of stress vs. strain and elongation at break refers to the amount a material stretches before it fractures or break. It is a measure of the flexibility or ductility of a material. The tensile strength, young's modulus and elongation at break results were observed for the developed film is shown in Fig. 4b-d. For CES and its composite films, the tensile strength values gradually escalate from 0.57 to 15.24. Elongation at break values also increases from 79.26 to 680.58 (CES to CES@CHI@CQDs). The respective value of young's modulus also comes out to be highest for the composite film i.e. CES@CHI@CQDs. These results indicating that the film's internal and external strength increases with the incorporation of chitosan and CQDs. The stronger interaction between polymer matrix and CQDs synergistically improves the overall mechanical property of the bioplastic film.

For ZMS and its bioplastic films, the values of tensile strength and elongation at break increases from 0.94 to 21.53 and 183.31 to 480.33 for (ZMS to ZMS@CHI@CQDs). The corresponding value of young's modulus was highest for ZMS@CHI@CQDs. By comparing the overall result, among all ZMS@CHI@CQDs has the highest value for tensile strength (21.53), Young's modulus (41). CES@CHI@CQDs achieved the maximum value for elongation at break value (680.58). The results interpreted from these findings suggested that the ZMS@CHI@CQDs exhibits maximum load bearing capacity as ZMS tensile strength values was more than CES (ZMS@0.935 - CES@0.573). Additionally, CES@CHI@CQDs has maximum flexibility because CES elongation at break values was higher than ZMS (ZMS@480.33 - CES@680.58).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Moisture content and water vapour permeability rate of CES@CHI@CQDs and ZMS@CHI@CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The moisture content and water vapour permeability rate for bioplastic films are most important parameters to consider in food packaging applications. From Fig. 5a, it can be illustrated that all the developed films possess some moisture content in them. This may be mainly explained due to the presence of glycerol, which is highly hydrophilic in nature and has surface moisture adsorption property. Among CES and its bioplastic films, CES possess highest moisture content value of ∼15 %. After the addition of CQDs, in the polymer matrix, 33.33 % decrement in the moisture content was observed. This decrease in the moisture content is mainly due to the crystallinity possessed by CQDs. CES@CHI, further helps in the improvement of moisture content as CHI is hydrophobic in nature and this leads to 48 % reduction in moisture compared with CES. The bioplastic film prepared from CES, CHI and CQDs has higher level of interaction among above mentioned films, therefore has the lowest value of moisture content and decline of 60 % in comparison to CES. Similarly, for ZMS and its bioplastic films, displayed a decrement in moisture content value. Pure ZMS attains moisture content value of 12 %, which is lower than CES, thus, supports the results of mechanical properties of prepared films. However, the decrement of 33.33 %, 56.25 % and 66.66 % were observed respectively for ZMS@CQDs, ZMS@CHI and ZMS@CQDs@CHI. This reduction in the percentage of moisture content is mainly due to hydrophobic nature of CHI and crystallinity possessed by CQDs.

Water vapour permeability rate is one of the most important parameter to consider for food packaging application. It explains the transmission of water vapour through the voids of polymer matrix. The values of WVTR showed significant reduction with the reinforcement of CHI and CQDs, in case of both CES and ZMS based films. This significant reduction corresponds to the filling of gaps by CHI and CQDs, ultimately leads to increase in the stability of prepared films. These results were found to be comparable with the data obtained from FESEM and mechanical properties. However, the CES@CHI@CQDs and ZMS@CHI@CQDs gives more compact structure, more bonding, highest hydrophobicity, less moisture contact, which clearly indicates the highest stability and lowest WVTR (Fig. 5b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water contact angle (WCA) of CES@CHI@CQDs and ZMS@CHI@CQD based films
ce:section.ce:para: The hydrophobicity of CES, ZMS and their integrated bioplastic films were investigated by carrying out their water contact angle (WCA) analysis from the surface of the films. In case of CES and its bioplastic films, there is an increment in the WCA by the addition of copolymer and functional filler. For instance, the WCA value for pure CES film was 44.46°. Although, the WCA value for CES@CQDs, CES@CHI and CES@CHI@CQDs was found to be 76.2°, 95° and 103.25° respectively (Fig. 6a-d). This disparity in the WCA values was related to the filling and aggregation of CQDs and CHI within the larger gaps in the starch network, which further prevented the penetration of water within the gaps of starch matrix. Similar results were shown for ZMS and its bioplastic films. For instance, the WCA value for ZMS, ZMS@CQDs, ZMS@CHI and ZMS@CHI@CQDs was turned out to be 47.25°, 89.85°, 95.4° and 115.4° respectively (Fig. 6e-h). These results were comparable with the results of mechanical properties, moisture contact angle and FESEM studies. The enhancement in the compactness by the inclusion of CHI and CQDs has led to the higher stability and hydrophobicity in the films. Therefore, ZMS@CHI@CQDs and CES@CHI@CQDs films possessed higher water resistance capacity and enhanced their potential usage in packaging applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial activities of bioplastic film films
ce:section.ce:para: The anti-bacterial activity of CES@CHI@CQDs and ZMS@CHI@CQD were examined against gram negative and gram positive bacterial strains i.e. E.coli and S.aureus (Fig. 7 ). The antibacterial studies are essential to investigate before employing the prepared films in packaging application for ensuring the food safety. The addition of chitosan into the bioplastic film has the ability to leverage its antibacterial properties. On comparing the results, it was observed that the films prepared from CES, ZMS, CES@CQDs and ZMS@CQDs have shown no antibacterial activity i.e. these films are highly biocompatible in nature. The susceptibility of CES@CHI, ZMS@CHI, CES@CHI@CQDs and ZMS@CHI@CQDs to both gram-positive and gram-negative bacterial strains displayed the antibacterial potential (evaluated by measuring the diameter of the inhibitory zones). For E.coli, the zone of inhibition for CES@CHI and CES@CHI@CQDs were 1 mm and 2 mm respectively. However, the values of zone of inhibition for ZMS@CHI and ZMS@CHI@CQDs were 1 mm and 2 mm. In case of S. aureus, the value of zone of inhibition for CES@CHI and CES@CHI@CQDs was 1 mm and 2 mm. The zone of inhibition was 2 mm and 4 mm for ZMS@CHI and ZMS@CHI@CQDs, respectively for S. aureus.

The optical and UV blocking properties are crucial to enhance the potential of films from external harmful UV radiations and ultimately elevates the shelf life of the prepared films. The UV–vis. and fluorescence spectral studies provide insight into the optical and UV blocking properties of the functional film (Fig. 8a and b). It was observed from the data that the aqueous solution of CQDs irradiate blue colour emission when exposed to UV light at λ = 365 nm. Additionally, UV light exposure cause an electronic transition corresponding to π-π⁎ and n-π⁎ in sp2 hybridised carbon atoms. However, the CQDs have shown the noticeable peak at 240 and 350 nm correspond to C = C as well as the C = O bond of the carboxyl group on the external surface of CQDs. Moreover, the prepared CQDs have also displayed the emission changes as a function of excitation wavelength in the range of 300–400 nm. Conclusively, the maximum excitation of CQDs has been observed at 330 nm. This observation confirmed that the outstanding optical and emission properties of synthesized CQDs further contribute in extending the shelf life of a product. The UV transmittance spectra of CQDs with different concentration are represented in Fig. 8c. Four different concentrations of CQDs were considered i.e. 1 %, 2 %, 3 % and 10 %. For, 1 wt% aqueous solution of CQDs has the ability to block UV-A radiations of 98.49 % and UV-B of 76.41 %. For 2 wt% CQDs, blocking percentage of UV-A was 98.5 % and UV-B was 84.04 %. The 5 wt% CQDs has possessed transmittance percentage of 99.79 % for UV-A and 93.44 % for UV-B respectively. However, when 10 wt% CQDs was exposed to UV radiations, UV-A blocked upto 99.96 % and UV-B blocked 96.03 % respectively. It is interpreted from the data that the blocking % of UV radiations has displayed linear relationship with increase in the percentages of CQDs. Thus, it acts as an efficient absorbent of UV-radiations, ultimately helps in increasing the overall shelf life of films.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antioxidant activities of CES@CHI@CQDs and ZMS@CHI@CQD based films
ce:section.ce:para: The antioxidant nature of the prepared bioplastic films i.e. CES, ZMS and their blends were analysed by DPPH radical scavenging assay (Fig. 8d). This property substantially enhances the shelf life of packed food items. The free radical scavenging efficiency of CES was found to be 1.5 %. However, the antioxidant property was found to be enhanced to 18 %, 45.5 % by the addition of CHI and CQDs in CES films. However, the value of % inhibition for CES@CHI@CQDs was 34 %. This result signifies that the CQDs played an important role for providing the antioxidant nature to the functional films. For ZMS and its composite films, similar trends were followed. For instance, the radical scavenging activity of ZMS, ZMS@CHI, ZMS@CQDs and ZMS@CHI@CQDs were 3 %, 20 %, 46 %, and 35 % respectively. In this case also, CQDs helps in improving the antioxidant nature of the bioplastic films. The results validated CQDs' potential role as a functional material in CES and ZMS films and expanded its application as a packaging material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biodegradability studies for of CES@CHI@CQDs and ZMS@CHI@CQD films
ce:section.ce:para: The higher biodegradability rate of bioplastics is a key driver for their potential usage over traditional plastics. The biodegradation test for Colocasia esculenta and Zea mays starch derived bioplastic films i.e. CES@CHI@CQDs and ZMS@CHI@CQDs were done by soil burial method. The whole study was performed for 28 days. After every 7th day, the weight of the bioplastic film was measured. In case of pure CES and ZMS, the degradation percentage is very high due to the hydrophilic nature of starch and glycerol. Also, these films were bio-compatible in nature, therefore microbial attack is dominant. On the 7th day, these films were found to be completely destroyed with around 90 % weight loss in both the films. However, the rate of degradation was found to be lower in case of CES@CHI and ZMS@CHI as compared with pure CES and ZMS films. This variation was mainly explained due to hydrophobic and antimicrobial nature of the chitosan and is confirmed from their antibacterial activity. On comparing the weight loss percentages, the CES@CHI exhibited 40.43 % weight loss, however, ZMS@CHI exhibited 45.94 % decrement in the weight. For CES@CQDs and ZMS@CQDs, the weight loss percentage was found to be 65 % and 63.14 % respectively. This loss in weight was found to be more than CES@CHI and ZMS@CHI and less than from pure CES and ZMS films. The variation from CES and ZMS was due to the stronger intermolecular interactions between starch and CQDs. However, the addition of CQDs has further supported the biocompatibility in developed films in comparison to CES@CHI and ZMS@CHI films. However, the minimum biodegradation rate was observed in CES@CHI@CQDs and ZMS@CHI@CQDs films. On comparing the two, the biodegradation rate of 25.25 % was found in CES@CHI@CQDs in contrast to 18 % in ZMS@CHI@CQDs. This variation was explained due to the collaborative effect of chitosan and CQDs in Colocasia esculenta and Zea mays starch derived bioplastic films. After 2 weeks, the weight loss % for CES@CHI and ZMS@CHI was 57.56 and 51.57 %. Similarly, the weight loss % for CES@CQDs, ZMS@CQDs, CES@CHI@CQDs and ZMS@CHI@CQDs were 65 %, 64.5 %, 37.27 %, 28.85 % respectively. On 21st day, the CES@CQDs and ZMS@CQDs based films were almost degraded with around 90 % weight loss. However, the weight loss % for CES@CHI, ZMS@CHI, CES@CHI@CQDs and ZMS@CHI@CQDs were 72.46 %, 68.53 %, 50.56 % and 46.73 % respectively. After 28th day, CES@CHI and ZMS@CHI were completely degraded. However, the CES@CHI@CQDs and ZMS@CHI@CQDs displayed weight loss of 48.58 % and 45.54 % respectively. After 2 month, CES@CHI@CQDs and ZMS@CHI@CQDs displayed complete degradation with no existence of films in the soil debris. Therefore, this outcome serves as evidence demonstrating the biodegradability and environmental friendliness of the prepared bioplastic films.

The effectiveness of food packaging applications is largely determined by the controlled release of additives from the packaging films. The release behviors of CQDs from the incorporated films is very essential. The release rate of CQDs under the exposure of different food stimulants is represented in Fig. 9 . Each profile show gradual stabilized increment upto 2 h and started declined behaviour after 2.5 h. The maximum release is shown in case of water followed by 10 %Ethanol. Additionally, minimum release is shown in case of 90 %Ethanol probably due to starch and chitosan insolubility in water. Furthermore, CES@CQDs and ZMS@CQDs exhibited higher release rate as compared to CES@CHI@CQDs and ZMS@CHI@CQDs due to latter's stronger polymer network, enhanced stability, and greater structural compactness. Besides the hydrophilic nature of CQDs, aqueous solutions are readily absorbed by the matrix, providing sufficient moisture to create a more open structure, which enhances the rapid release of CQDs. Conversely, alcohols exhibit limited interaction with Chi/St polymers, leading to the entrapment of CQDs within the matrix, thereby reducing their diffusion and mobility within the confined structure (32). As a result, compared to alcoholic solutions, CQDs were released more rapidly and in greater quantities in water.

The performance of CES@CHI@CQDs and ZMS@CHI@CQDs were further assessed towards the packaging of lychee. For the analysis, similar type of lychees were chosen and further packed in CES@CHI@CQDs and ZMS@CHI@CQDs films (Fig. 10 ). The changes in the appearance of the fruit were analysed using digital photograph. For the initial 2 days, there was no observable change in the appearance of chosen fruit either in the control or the fruit wrapped in biofilms. However, after 3 days, the fruit started deteriorating in case of control. On the other hand, the freshness of fruit remains intact in case CES@CHI@CQDs and ZMS@CHI@CQDs wrapped fruit. After 4 days, the colour of pericarp of control becomes black. However, the freshness of CES@CHI@CQDs and ZMS@CHI@CQDs packed fruit remained still intact. After 7th days, the control fruit exhibited extensive rotting and emitted a foul odor, whereas the fruit covered in the biofilm displayed little browning over the external pericarp of lychee fruit. On 10th day, the lychee fruit under control condition becomes extremely smelly, juice exudation and bacterial spots were shown. However, the CES@CHI@CQDs and ZMS@CHI@CQDs enclosed fruit become black. The results interpreted from the data that in control fruit, smell, juice exudation and bacterial spots were observed due to 100 % of water permeability and no protection from bacterial colonies whereas in case of fruit wrapped in bioplastic film, exhibited better preservation performance for lychee fruit for ten days. Further, the biodegradable nature of prepared films has the potential to manage carbon footprints and alleviate the periling aptitude of plastic.

The current work highlights the fabrication of Colocasia esculenta and Zea mays starch derived bioplastic films with the reinforcement of chitosan and CQDs. The results indicated that the incorporation of chitosan and CQDs helps in improving the mechanical, thermal, optical, hydrophobicity, antioxidant and antibacterial property in Colocasia esculenta and Zea mays starch derived bioplastic films. Among all the resulting biofilms, ZMS@CHI@CQDs and CES@CHI@CQDs provides the best results. The tensile strength and elongation at break value escalate from 0.57 to 15.24 and 79.26 to 680.58 in case of CES and 0.94 to 21.53 and 183.3 to 480.33 for ZMS composite films. The water contact angle also increases from 44.26° to 103.25° (CES to CES@CHI@CQDs) and 47.25° to 115.4° (ZMS to ZMS@CHI@CQDs). Chitosan provides antibacterial nature to the films and CQDs induces antioxidant and UV barrier properties. The bioplastic films of CES@CHI@CQDs and ZMS@CHI@CQDs also hold significant potential for applications in active food packaging. The characteristic feature of biodegradability assessed through loam interment methodology further supported the potential usage of these prepared films in packaging. The antibacterial, anti-oxidative and UV-blocking abilities of formed films enhanced their scope in industrial sector. The biodegradable nature of prepared films has the potential to manage carbon footprints and alleviate the periling aptitude of plastic.

Manmeet Kour: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Savita Chaudhary: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Rajeev Kumar: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Software, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Manmeet Kour is thankful to Maulana Azad National Fellowship (MANF). Savita Chaudhary is thankful to DST Inspire Faculty award [IFACH- 17], Haryana State Council for Science and Technology [HSCSIT/R&D/2020/476], DST Chandigarh [S&T&RE/RP/147/e-2873/(19-20)/Sanc/12/2019/1878-1885], and [S&T&RE/RP/147/e-2873/(22-23)/Sanc/09/2022/907-915] and DST Purse grants II for financial assistance. Rajeev Kumar would like to acknowledge the support of UGC, India under the INDO-US 21st Century knowledge Initiative project [F.No. 194-2/2016 (IC)]. The authors are thankful to the Sophisticated Analytical Instrumentation Facility (SAIF, CIL), Panjab University, Chandigarh for technical support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2025.142996.
